data.title: Hubbell Strongly Leveraged To Electrification Growth, But Also Priced Accordingly
data.description:
Hubbell Incorporated's (HUBB) performance since my last update on this supplier of electrical and utility components has been interesting. Utility demand has been positive overall (outside of metering), and data center demand has been strong, but overall financial performance has tracked slightly below my expectations. Even so, the company remains well-placed to leverage secular growth trends like “electrify everything”, reshoring, utility upgrade cycles, and data center capex.

Hubbell shares are up a little less than 50% since that last article, basically tracking in line with the larger industrial sector and likewise matching the performance of other electrification plays like Rexel (RXEEY) (RXLSF) and Schneider (SBGSY), while slightly underperforming Eaton (ETN) and more significantly underperforming nVent (NVT).

I do think that Hubbell is a differentiated growth story in the industrial space with attractive growth and margin leverage opportunities over the next five-plus years. I also think, though, that the market largely appreciates it as that, and it’s hard for me to argue that the shares are significantly undervalued.

Reacceleration In Q4, But Can They Finally Meet (Let Alone Beat) Expectations?

One of the issues that pops up in looking at Hubbell’s recent performance is the string of top-line misses that stretches back to the second quarter of 2022. These haven’t been big misses (often around 2%) and you could perhaps blame sell-side analysts for errors in modeling, but the reality is that Hubbell management themselves have lowered guidance along the way as well.

This creates an interesting setup for the upcoming fourth quarter report. On one hand, management is guiding for a significant rebound in organic growth – from around 3% in Q3’25 to 8% to 10% in Q4 and a level of growth that should have the company in the top ranks of organic growth among the industrials I follow. On the other hand, can they actually come in at or above expectations and carry some sentiment momentum into 2026?

On the positive side, utility spending remains healthy, with distribution now joining transmission and substation as areas of growth/strength in the utility market. Data center spending also remains robust, with most companies with exposure to this vertical (electrical components or otherwise) seeing better than expected results. I also think that one of the main drags in recent quarters – metering and AMI – will be less of a drag; not because the business is substantially stronger, but because the year-ago comp is quite a bit easier (Grid Automation revenue was down 11% in last year’s fourth quarter, with metering/AMI pulling down growth in grid protection/controls).

I’m guardedly bullish that Hubbell will deliver growth in the 9%-9.5% range for the quarter and set the stage for a healthy 5% to 6% revenue growth guide for FY’26 (likely a range of 4% to 6%). I’m less concerned about margins, as Hubbell has done comparatively better here, and I think the company will see more than a point of adjusted operating margin leverage (to around 23%).

Drivers In Place For Above-Average Long-Term Growth

While most industrial companies target 3% to 4% long-term core revenue growth, the reality is that many end up more in the 2%-3% range. I expect Hubbell to be different, as the company is well-positioned to leverage above-average multiyear growth in electrical and utility spending.

Utility grid spending seems unlikely to slow meaningfully in the foreseeable future. A meaningful amount of spending (over a third of distribution spending and closer to a quarter of transmission spending) is going towards grid hardening, but there is still ongoing need for expansion spending to accommodate renewables generation and to address the increasing demands of increasingly electrified commercial buildings and factories, not to mention electricity-hungry data centers.

Above and beyond that, utilities continue to invest in grid protection, monitoring, and controls to not only protect assets from malicious attacks, but also disruptions from natural hazards. Moreover, automation not only improves customer satisfaction (avoiding or mitigating power disruptions, or at least fixing them faster), but also reduces operating costs, freeing up resources to reinvest in hardware needs.

Hubbell is also well-placed to benefit from ongoing manufacturing reshoring and electrification. Although reshoring has been much more significant in industries that already needed to add capacity (like semiconductors and communications equipment), it is still happening, and guidance from major automation providers like Rockwell (ROK) points to more growth on the way.

These new factories tend to be much more automated and electrified than in the past, driving demand for both Hubbell’s industrial products (like power quality systems, wiring systems, controls, et al.) and utility systems, as new factories often require utility-level components like substation switches. Additionally, companies continue to invest in electrification retrofits, whether to replace outdated and inefficient generation systems or to facilitate more automation within the facility.

Last and not least is data center capex growth. I don’t believe management has ever quantified the company’s exposure to this market, but it’s enough to move the needle for the Electrical Systems segment, and the company offers a range of products like cabinets, grounding systems, enclosures, cable management systems, and integrated units. With Eaton recently talking about a six to nine-year pipeline for DC projects at a sell-side conference, this can be a meaningful ongoing contributor to growth.

The Outlook

I’m relatively confident that utility spending will be healthy in 2026 (outside of metering), and I’m likewise confident in the ongoing growth in data center capex. I’m not as confident about the outlook for industrial and non-resi end-markets, though I think both should improve as the year progresses. I’m expecting over 6% growth next year, over 6% growth over the next three years, and long-term annualized growth of a little over 5%.

On the margins, I’ve been impressed with how management has opportunistically leveraged cost inflation to its long-term benefit. In the past, management has raised prices in response to cost input inflation (typically metals), but then held those prices even when costs abated. That has helped drive EBITDA margins from the mid-teens to the low/mid-20%’s, and I think input cost inflation and tariffs in 2025 could well set the stage for a repeat in the years to come (though not at quite the same magnitude).

I’m expecting adjusted EBITDA margin to exceed 25% in FY’26 and improve to 27%-28% in the years to come, helping drive free cash flow margins into the high-teens and high-single-digit FCF growth.

Discounted cash flow doesn’t suggest a lot of undervaluation now (mid-single-digit prospective returns), and that’s not unusual for “theme” stocks.

Turning to a multiples-based approach, I can argue for 17.5x forward multiple on the basis of the company’s profitability and above-average revenue growth potential, but “potential” is a tricky word and there will almost certainly be multiple compression if Hubbell comes up short on growth or margins. I’d also note that a 17.5x multiple doesn’t exactly support a robust premium at a fair value of around $485.

The Bottom Line

I want to like Hubbell more than I do. I think the revenue growth and margin leverage story is legit, and I think 2026 will see better results. I’ve also made it clear in the past that I really like electrification as a long-term theme, and I think Hubbell is a quality “picks and shovels” play. All of that said, valuation still matters, and I just can’t get comfortable with the valuation/modeling gymnastics it would take to get to a truly exciting fair value today.

data.title: Things That Can't Go On Forever...
data.description:
If something cannot go on forever, it will stop." - Herb Stein

Howard Marks, a co-founder of Oaktree Capital Management put out a sage observation earlier this week.

AI is currently the subject of great enthusiasm. If that enthusiasm doesn’t produce a bubble conforming to the historical pattern, that will be a first. "

This is a view I have articulated a few times on these pages over the past twelve months. The stock market feels like a 1999 redux. Just like that era, a major technology paradigm shift is driving both the equity markets and the economy. Back at the turn of the 20th century, it was the birth of the internet, now it is the AI Revolution. This huge surge of technology related spending is boosting GDP growth to a large extent.

There are some major differences between 1999 and 2026, however. Back in 1999, the U.S. was the unquestioned and only global power on the planet. Russia was in shambles after losing the Cold War and the subsequent dissolution of the Soviet Union. Hundreds of millions of people in China were still mired in poverty, and the nation had not yet joined the WTO and subsequently became the world's workshop.

In 1999, the Baby Boomers, the largest demographic cohort in U.S. history, were in their prime earning years. This year, the oldest Baby Boomers turn 80 years old. Most are retired or close to retirement and passing from this Earth at an accelerating rate. Consumer spending was also less bifurcated. The top 10% of households by income now accounts for roughly 50% of all consumer spending.

From a fiscal perspective, 2026 and 1999 are like night and day. In 1999, the U.S. ran a small fiscal surplus, and the overall national debt was less than $6 trillion. This fiscal year the U.S. will run an approximate $2 trillion fiscal deficit again. Federal debt has surpassed the $38 trillion and the debt to GDP ratio has roughly doubled since 1999.

And unfortunately, the rest of the G-7 is also struggling with their debt loads and have lower GDP growth rates to boot. Not to mention even lower birth/replacement rates.

In addition to being powered by a major technology paradigm shift, 1999 and 2026 have this in common. A large portion of this surge in tech spending is being powered by what was called 'vendor financing' in the late 1990s. Of course, the spending powered by the likes of Cisco Systems (CSCO), Global Crossing and Lucent pales in comparison to the current vendor financing ecosystem.

OpenAI which should deliver approximately $14 billion in revenues in FY2025, and is bleeding cash, has nonetheless just over $1.4 trillion in commitments in future computing capacity from the likes of Oracle (ORCL). The company is going to have to raise a huge amount of additional capital at current cash burn rates to meet those commitments. This is why I recently articulated that a huge and successful IPO of OpenAI would be one of the most positive signposts I could envision happening in 2026.

So, despite much more favorable tailwinds across many fronts in 1999, the bubble still popped in early 2000. Before the Internet Bust meltdown finally bottomed in the late summer of 2002, the NASDAQ fell just over 80% from peak to trough. The S&P 500 lost over 40%.

And investors should acknowledge that are far as valuations go, we are firmly in the end of the Internet Boom territory. This doesn't mean all stocks are substantially overvalued, even some of the tech giants that have led this advance over the past three years.

Given the projected earnings and sales growth rates for Alphabet (GOOG), paying 31 times forward earnings doesn't put the stock in value or even GARP territory in my view. That said, there are plenty of names like Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) trading at 250 times forward earnings and nearly 100 times revenues, are practically screaming Bubble!

Let's take a look at some historical valuation metrics to compare the last innings of the Internet Boom in 1999 to the current equity market. They are very similar. One metric, the 'Buffett Indicator' shows today's market is substantially more overvalued than the tail end of the Internet Boom.

The Shiller PE ratio is flashing at least yellow right now as well.

In addition, unlike in generations past, almost all the gains from equity investments come from price appreciation. Little comes from dividend payments like in the past. This is why stock buybacks have become so important not only for the C-Suite making their EPS targets for equity and option awards, but for investors as well. Dividend yields also used to help to put floors under stock market declines.

Another cautionary reading is coming from corporate insiders, who are not stepping up to buy much in the market judging from the current insider buying/selling ratio.

I will end this look at market valuations, with the price to sales ratio of the S&P 500. This metric has only been higher at the tail end of the Internet Boom. This ratio is significantly higher than at the end of 2021. 2022 saw the S&P 500 fall just over 18% and the NASDAQ lose roughly one third of its value.

All this said, valuations are probably not going to be the trigger for the popping of the AI Narrative. Historically, valuations haven't been the pin that has pricked the bubble. However, extreme valuation levels do make the subsequent bear market much more painful whenever the inevitable occurs. In one of my next articles, I will try to tackle the most likely trigger for the eventual popping of the current market bubble.

In the meantime, the combination of tax cuts, tariff easing, and lower interest rates is likely going to push the market still higher. At least in the first half of 2026. The revision to GDP growth in Q4 earlier this week was certainly impressive. Therefore, I continue to hold 25% of my portfolio in short term treasuries and some cash to have 'dry powder' to deploy whenever the next 'hiccup' in the market occurs.

However, the rest of my portfolio is within covered call positions around the few stocks in the market that still sport reasonable valuations. Not an exciting configuration, but this strategy returned roughly 17% in both 2024 and 2025 for my portfolio, while taking much less risk than straight buy and hold investing.

data.title: Deutsche Bank's Comeback: Low Valuation, Rising Profits, Strong Capital Position
data.description:
European banks are some of the top-performing stocks during the current bull market. The iShares MSCI Europe Financials ETF (EUFN) has returned more than 63% in just the past 12 months, easily outperforming the State Street Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF). Deutsche Bank AG (DB), specifically, has been a star across the pond. Shares of the now $74 billion market cap bank have more than doubled since January 2025.

I have a "Buy" rating on the stock. I see DB as reasonably valued, while a long-term uptrend following a decade-long malaise augurs for higher prices in the year ahead.

DB Leads the European Bank Pack, Alpha to U.S. Banks YoY

Back in October, DB reported a solid set of quarterly results. Its net income summed to €1.77 billion, up 5% sequentially and better than 10% above consensus, according to BofA, despite a YoY drop tied to one-off items. The Q3 details generally highlighted that the bank was on track to deliver on its 2025 plan. Revenue verified at €8.0 billion, up 3% from Q3 and 7% above year-ago levels.

Shares jumped 4.1% in the session that followed, marking a third consecutive post-earnings climb. Ahead of the Jan. 29 Q4 report, the options market prices in a modest 4.9% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the numbers hit the tape. Implied volatility is notable, but not extreme, at 30.1%, with the next dividend date slated for May 26.

Looking closer at the quarter that was, DB targets €32 billion for FY 2025 revenue and more than 10% return on tangible equity. For the Q4 report, it’s possible that the bank reduced its loan loss provisions, which may boost the bottom line. Adding to the sanguine backdrop, there really hasn't been much in the way of exposure to any material credit events.

2025 financial targets and capital objectives

Investment banking trends were solid, helped by strong M&A, particularly in the healthcare sector and within the AI niche. Its FIC and macro products helped the top line, while private banking and asset management also showed some sequential strength. The balance sheet remains in good shape—the CET1 ratio rose to 14.5%, above street estimates.

Significant progress in divisional strategies

For the shareholders, DB completed a €1 billion share buyback program and reaffirmed plans to return more than €8 billion in distributions to shareholders for 2022–2026—a clear sign of optimism that the long-term strategy is on solid ground. The management team was a bit cautious about geopolitical risks on the earnings call, though, so any macro hiccups in Europe could cause a stock correction. Then, at its November Capital Markets Day, the bank set a 2028 revenue CAGR target of over 5%.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to jump by more than 25% this year, which should result in a much higher dividend payout based on its distribution policy and previously provided guidance from management. The yield could jump above 3% if the stock consolidates or drops from here, assuming no adverse changes in the banking industry.

DB is not widely covered by the street, but there has been an upgrade in the past 90 days with no downgrades. Profitability trends are sublime, according to Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. In fact, DB now ranks No. 8 out of 684 in the Financials sector.

Deutsche Bank: Revenue & Earnings Outlook, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, the stock still appears to me as a decent valuation, even after the massive rally from under $10 in March 2023 to just shy of $40 recently. If we assume $4.53 of current-year EPS and apply a conservative 10x bank multiple, then shares should trade near $45.

DB also trades below book value on a trailing 12-month basis, which can’t be said of many major U.S. banks.

DB: Low P/E and P/B, Solid Dividend Yield

Key risks for DB include instability in the EU, which could result in slower banking activity and a deterioration in the firm’s balance sheet. What’s more, while capital markets activity appears to be on a favorable trajectory, global GDP weakness would be a negative catalyst for dealmaking and DB sales and profits.

Deutsche Bank has also been at the center of regulatory scrutiny and litigation in previous cycles. Specifically, the private credit space bears watching both at home and abroad as 2026 progresses.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Thursday, Jan. 29, BMO. The company hosts its quarterly fixed income call the following day.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares having been on an incredible run in the past few years, the bulls would appear to be in control of the primary trend. The technical situation backs that up. Notice in the chart below that the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, while shares coil around the 50dma.

I took a long-term view, and we find that there isn’t a lot of resistance up to the 2012 high in the mid-$50s. So, I would not be surprised to see the stock increase by another 50% or so.

In the here and now, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph is mildly concerning. A bearish momentum divergence has emerged, with price making new highs while the RSI notches lower highs. It’s possible that DB tags the 200dma in the months ahead, while still keeping the long-term bullish trend in place.

I’d call out that the upside measured move price objective based on the 2016 through 2024 basing pattern was achieved and then some.

DB: Long-Term Base & Breakout, Mid-$50s Resistance

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on DB. It’s both a value play (with a low P/E and P/B) and a growth idea (given high net income advances ongoing). The chart, meanwhile, appears strong with little overhead resistance ahead of the Jan. 29, Q4 earnings report.

data.title: Target Hospitality: Aiming In The Right Direction Towards Growth
data.description:
Four months since my previous coverage, Target Hospitality Corp. (TH) has stayed flat and slow-moving after decreasing by 9.9%. While this contradicts my bullish view of this stock, I perfectly understand the cautious market stance. After all, it is still on its way towards complete recovery from contract cancellations with South Texas Family Residential Center and Pecos Children’s Center. Despite this, its resilient operations and robust fundamentals still promise sustainability and longevity. Valuation remains attractive, while technicals show some buying opportunities.

Target Hospitality: How It Has Been Recently

Ever since my very first article on Target Hospitality Corp., I have discussed how it took a nosedive after the contract cancellations from Q3 2024 to Q1 2025. Yet, I noted its resilience as it tried to regain its footing and open itself to another niche. And this started to pay off, as we noted in its most recent performance.

During Q3 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $99.4M, up by 4.4% YoY from $95.2M. This was also the first time it had positive revenue growth in seven quarters. This shows its rebound attempt from its weakness in my first two coverages. It’s true that it has not yet completely recovered from the impact of contract cancellations. Services income and specialty rental income remained weaker on a YoY basis. This was most visible across various services or segments, such as government, hospitality, and facilities services. Yet, its construction fee income completely offset them. Its entry into workforce hospitality solutions due to the Workforce Hub contract drove it.

However, inflationary headwinds remained a primary challenge for TH as cost pressures intensified. Its service costs more than doubled, which raised its operating costs and expenses. The only consolation is that the other segments stayed manageable. It was also good that its revenues were still big enough to cover them. As a result, its operating margin plunged, but was still positive at 0.06%. It was a huge improvement from -28% in my previous coverage.

Challenges

Inflationary headwinds are still intense, and even TH is not completely insulated from their unfavorable impact. Currently, US inflation is 2.7% and has already weakened from its 14-month high of 3.0%. However, positive inflation means that prices across the country are still increasing. This affects the cost pressures of the company. One factor is the labor cost, since it may require higher wages or compensation as living costs increase. Prices of food and supplies may rise, which can affect the cost of providing meals to workforce lodging sites.

Another impact of inflation lies in materials and maintenance, particularly building materials like steel, lumber, and roofing. It must also watch out for the impact of new tariffs, since exporters of building materials may raise prices to pass them on to domestic suppliers and other related companies.

Mitigants/Drivers

Despite these challenges, I believe that TH is strongly positioned today.

One aspect to consider is the contractual nature of its business. It's true that TH has contractual limitations on passing through extra cost pressures to its customers or clients. However, this contractual nature protects it against macroeconomic volatility. Since contracts are typically long-term and/or fixed-priced, they secure a stable revenue stream for a definite and long period. It knows what and how to prepare to keep and attract current and potential clients.

Additionally, its contracts are typically with the government and/or large clients, which can help it cushion short-term cost spikes. That way, it doesn't have to constantly reprice to consumers. This is a typical problem faced by typical retail hospitality businesses. More interestingly, it serves companies and their workforce within natural resource or energy sectors like oil and gas. These groups are more elastic to discretionary consumer spending. This is opposite to traditional hospitality businesses like hotels and apartments that rely heavily on leisure travel.

Another thing that may continue to drive growth is its continued expansion and customer diversification. In my previous coverage, I've already noted the strong potential of its data center contract exposure. Last November, it announced its expansion in this niche by 160%. From a 250-bed data center community, it now aims to support an extra 400 beds for 650 individuals. Initially, this expansion is expected to generate about $40M of committed minimum revenue through March 2028. Additionally, this expansion contract has four one-year extensions through March 2032. On top of that, TH is set to construct and provide hospitality services to support the expansion of mining and data center development in Northern Nevada. Collectively called Power Community, this may lead to $35M revenue over the 25-month contract period to support 250 individuals. Considering these trends, TH now has a larger regional presence with a well-diversified customer base.

Most importantly, TH can sustain its increasing operating capacity. Its robust liquidity is evident in its cash flow statement. Because its operations became profitable last quarter, its operating cash flow rose substantially from my previous coverage. At $53.4M, it was able to cover capex and debt repayments and led to a net cash inflow of $11M. As a result, its current cash and cash equivalents are now $30. Meanwhile, it has already zeroed out its borrowings. With that, TH is now debt-free with decent cash levels. This proves that TH has enough capital buffer today.

Valuation/Projection

Because of its improved performance last quarter, I felt the need to update my projection. Also, the cumulative revenue for three quarters is already $230.9B. This is already very close to my initial estimation of $243B. After updating it based on new quarterly trends, my new estimate is $298M. For its operating costs (55%) and expenses (14%), I set them as a percentage of revenue based on recent quarterly trends. Capex and depreciation are the same, since I still used the straight-line method. As a result, the ending NOPAT will decrease. But I think this is more logical considering the substantial increase in the operating costs and expenses.

In line with my new projection, my DCF Model will also be affected. You can see that the FCFF also decreased. I still set the perpetual growth rate at 2.0% to be conservative and consistent with my previous model. Meanwhile, WACC increased to 11.15% because I increased its risk premium to 7.0% to price in more risks. I also set its Beta at 1.0, assuming TH to be in line with the S&P 500. Lastly, debt is already zero. As a result, my new TP or target price will be $10.79. If I add a 5% risk consideration, it will become $10.24. Either way, TH still appears reasonable at its current price.

Lastly, I assessed it using the P/B Ratio. The stock trades at 1.99x its book value, versus the five-year average of 3.18x. It is also trading at its cheapest level since 2020. Using these to derive the fair value, the TP will be $12.84.

Technicals

TH’s technicals weakened after its rally in the past two months. Yet, the price still shows a possibility of a rebound as it moves above the 50-day and 200-day SMA. This means that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. Also, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, which shows that market sentiments are still generally optimistic.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI explain the recent price weakness. The MACD line is trending below the signal line as selling volume increases. The histogram is within negative territory, which implies that selling volume has already outpaced buying volume. However, RSI shows that this was mainly due to the recent overbuying and profit-taking. Now, investors may see new buying opportunities as the price strength normalizes.

Conclusion

Target Hospitality Corp. keeps rebounding from the challenges it has faced in the past year. It’s not completely insulated from headwinds, but its strategic business model and expansion allow it to mitigate risks, drive growth, and sustain its capacity. Valuation is also reasonable, as the TP suggests some upside. Technicals are weaker due to recent overbuying and profit-taking but show new entry opportunities. I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Cal-Maine Foods: Prepared Better Than Ever For This Brutal Downcycle
data.description:
Intro

In my previous analysis, about a month and a half ago, on Cal-Maine Foods, Inc. (CALM), I wrote:

The year-to-date chart shows even better that the $80–85 level is a key support area to hold; if it breaks and closes below, the likelihood of the stock going to test the next support level in the $70–73 area is high.

With the downside scenario having played out, aided by a nationwide collapse in wholesale egg prices, and the stock now hovering around $73, it feels like a good moment to reassess CALM’s prospects to see if the $70 level can hold as support both technically and fundamentally, and decide whether the current price is a solid opportunity to accumulate or if it’s better to wait for even lower levels.

Since I covered CALM’s longer journey in more detail in the last analysis, this time I’m focusing on the latest dynamics, as the company has just released their fiscal Q2 earnings.

Recent Dynamics

I think a great introduction to Cal‑Maine’s recent developments is a twist on an old saying: sometimes a single graph can say more than a thousand words.

Wholesale egg prices over the past 10 years:

The latest lows were last seen seven years ago, in 2019.

Stock Price Dependence on Egg Prices

When monitoring Cal‑Maine stock, I think it’s always been a good idea to keep an eye on wholesale egg prices as well. While the company has worked hard to reduce its reliance on conventional egg pricing and made great progress on that, around half of its revenue is still tied to it.

Looking at the correlation between CALM and egg prices over the past five years, one could say that a historical divergence became evident at the start of this year. Wholesale egg prices, after hitting as high as $8 per dozen in February, plunged to $3 by the end of March. March 2025 could be seen as a historic moment for CALM, as the stock price defied the drop in egg prices, reversed course, and reached an all-time as high around $120 by the end of July 2025.

The chart highlights the trends, with blue showing the CALM stock price and green representing the national average wholesale price for a dozen eggs:

I believe the CALM share price might have held up better and kept its strong uptrend if egg prices had stayed in the $1.50 to $2 range. Instead, they’ve pretty much collapsed — wholesale prices have recently dropped to around $0.45. All things considered, I think the stock price has actually held up quite well so far after such a dramatic plunge in egg prices.

Q2 Earnings

Earnings were mixed, with weak top line performance offset by a strong bottom line:

  • Net sales came in at $769.5 million, down 19.4%, missing expectations by $57 million.
  • EPS was $2.13, down 52.3%, but still topped expectations by $0.19.

Although somewhat unusually highlighted in green during the earnings presentation, other profitability metrics also showed a noticeable drop compared to last year with only H1 FY26 cash flow showing year-over-year growth:

Despite weak revenue and profitability comparisons due to last year’s high egg prices, much-needed progress was reported in reducing CALMs commodity-like dependence on conventional egg prices:

Egg Sales Breakdown:

  • Shell egg sales: $649.6 million, down 28.1%
    • Driven by 26.5% lower prices and 2.2% lower volume.
  • Conventional eggs: $363.9 million, down 41.0%
    • Prices down 38.8%, volume down 3.6%.
  • Specialty eggs: $285.7 million, down just 0.4%
    • Prices dipped 0.8%, but volume actually rose 0.3%.

Capital Allocation

Shareholders got some good news as management shared updates on capital allocation, pointing out a boost in stock buybacks after almost no transactions in Q1.

Key Takeaways from Management’s Discussion on the Earnings Call

GLP-1 tailwinds

Consumers are seeking complete high-quality proteins. GLP-1 users are gravitating towards satisfying nutrient-dense foods, younger consumers and families are treating eggs as an everyday staple.

We see consumers trading up. Specialty and premium segments are showing stronger repeat usage and alignment with the attributes people care about: wellness, taste, simplicity and clean labels. Put simply, eggs are leading on health, convenience and quality, and that combination is reshaping category growth in a way that we believe plays directly to our strengths.

Prepared foods business

..we are positioning our prepared foods business to deliver sustained double-digit volume growth.

When you combine Echo Lake and Crepini, we expect total prepared foods capacity to increase more than 30% over the next 18 to 24 months. We believe this will position us to meet accelerating demand for high-protein, ready-to-eat, convenience forward formats that are aligned with changing consumer preferences.

..we're confident that we can continue over the long term to grow that business is what we call out when we acquired Echo Lake at that 9% to 10% CAGR.

When questioned about the nearly flat year-over-year Q2 volume of specialty eggs, touted as a growth segment, the CFO responded:

we're making a very tough comparison. If you remember, conventional eggs became extremely tight that put a lot of demand on specialty eggs. So to be flat is a huge win

we believe we can have double-digit specialty growth over time. So despite very difficult market comparison, we still have a lot of confidence in where our specialty business is and where it's going.

Management discussions about bird flu

if you look at what we think is a greater indicator and that's just presence of the virus, it's an absolute terrible situation. It's all over the U.S. And in bigger than that, it's all over the globe.

So the presence of the virus is extremely strong. 2025 was the worst year ever at 45.6 million layers in pullets that exceeded the next worst year of 2022 of $43.1 million.

Shifting from a highly cyclical business model to one that’s steadier and more predictable.

In addition to accelerating value-added prepared foods, we're growing specialty eggs. In the second quarter, we acquired certain production assets Clean Egg LLC in Texas, which expands our specialty cage-free and free-range egg capacity and supports local sourcing, captures accelerating market growth and optimizes our supply chain. These investments are expected to have strengthened our mid-cycle earnings profile and build a more resilient business over time. They also reinforce what makes Cal-Maine unique among agriculture producers.

Valuation

Since my last valuation modeling was done just a month and a half ago, I see no reason to change it, even with the recent quarter's weaker free cash flow metric, which, despite the conventional egg price crash, still came in at around $50 million positive.

Based on the previous analysis:

My calculations showed a 5-year average free cash flow of $446.1 million. Using a conservative multiple of 7, which seems fairly appropriate for a commodity-linked cyclical company, the implied enterprise value comes to about $3.12 billion. Adding $1.25 billion in cash and short-term investments from the balance sheet, and with 48.42 million diluted shares outstanding last quarter, I came up with a conservative estimate of the fair stock value at $90.37 per share.

While cash and short-term investments dipped slightly to $1.14 billion, the stock count also fell to 48.17 million from buyback transactions, which roughly offset the drop in the cash balance.

Risks

In my view the primary risks stem from bird flu and similar epidemics. Still, as the industry leader, Cal‑Maine appears better positioned than most smaller competitors to manage these challenges and has historically maintained strong profitability during past outbreaks.

Another risk to consider is the possibility that the recent egg price crash persists, which could keep CALM’s profitability under pressure for an extended period.

The third key risk concerns the ongoing price‑fixing investigations. They are significant, but the process is lengthy and may take years, with no certainty about how it will ultimately unfold. From latest SEC filing: Management cannot predict the eventual scope, duration or outcome of these investigations and is unable to estimate the amount or range of potential losses, if any, at this time.

Takeaway

I think wrapping up with CEO Sherman Miller’s remark from the earnings call is a great idea, as it perfectly captures the company’s current view on the tough cycle:

..this is a fundamentally different company than the last time we experienced similar market conditions. Today, we have a stronger balance sheet, meaningful growth, both organically and through acquisitions, greater diversification into specialty eggs and prepared foods, deeper bench strength across the organization and reduced exposure to pure commodity pricing through specialty mix, hybrid pricing models and value-added products. We are more diversified and more resilient and better positioned to compound value over the long term.

I’m sticking with my Buy rating for CALM, but given the recent moves in both the stock and egg prices, caution feels like the best way to describe the current setup. The stock's technicals are broken, and it may take some time for the stock to establish a stronger footing. That said, as management pointed out, the bird flu situation in wildlife is dire, and any new outbreak among egg producers could add even more volatility to the stock price.

data.title: British American Tobacco: FY26 Outlook Points To 10%+ Total Shareholder Yield
data.description:
BAT reaffirmed 2026 guidance

My last covered British American Tobacco p.l.c. (BTI) on Nov 18 with an article titled of "Q3 Tobacco Dividend Roundup: Altria Outshines British American Tobacco”. That article placed an emphasis on the dividends of BTI and rated it as buy.

Since then, a new development that is noteworthy is the release of its FY25 Trading Update and also update of its FY 2026 guidance. In this update, management reaffirmed their medium-term growth targets in the range of 3% to 5% on revenue and 5% to 8% on adjusted EPS. For reasons to be elaborated on later, I believe these growth rates, combined with the stock’s current valuation, presented a biased return/risk ratio. The return/risk ratio is further skewed by a few more specifics as quoted below from the press release:

BAT FY25 update and 2026 guidance (Dec 9, 2025):

  • Further acceleration in New Category contribution, in line with our Quality Growth approach
  • Confident in sustainably delivering mid-term growth algorithm from 2026 (+3-5% revenue, +4-6% APFO and +5-8% adj. diluted EPS), with 2026 performance expected at the lower end of the range
  • Strong cash generation and balanced capital allocation; on track to reduce leverage to within 2.0-2.5x by end 2026, alongside progressive dividends and sustainable share buy-backs – FY26: £1.3bn.

Next, I will detail why these updates point to total shareholder yield (“TSY”) exceeding 10%.

BTI share repurchases: historical perspective and outlook

To better contextualize this discussion, let’s first take a look at the company’s share repurchase program in recent years. As seen, BIT has become significantly more active on this front over the last two to three years. In 2022, the company stopped issuing new shares and executed a sizable repurchase of $2,229 million. The activities slowed a bit in 2023 to about $140 million only but resumed an upward trend afterwards. This activity accelerated significantly in 2024 and 2025, with repurchases jumping to $991.6 million in 2024 and more than $1,158 million as of TTM 2025. With the above update, BTT now expects £1.3bn share buyback for FY26, translating into about $1.74 billion at today’s exchange rate and roughly doubling the buyback in 2024.

Such buyback programs signal improved/improving cash flow and also capital flexibility in my model. At the same time, they also add a meaningful vector to the TSY via net common buyback yield (see the next chart below). As seen in the cashflow statement below, BTI has historically paid generous dividends to common shareholders. The current dividend yield is about 5.67% as of this writing. With its FY2026 buyback guidance of $1.74 billion and its current market cap of $117 billion, the implied net buyback yield would be around 1.49% for 2026. Thus, from its cash dividend and buyback, shareholders could expect an effective yield of 7.16% already.

BTI’s deleverage efforts

But there is yet another yield vector judging by the FY2026 guidance. If you recall, management is confident about reducing the company’s leverage to within 2.0-2.5x by end 2026 thanks to the strong cash generation and balanced capital allocation.

To better contextualize these figures, let’s again set a historical background first. To wit, the next chart shows BTI’s latest cash flow statement. From the cash flow statements, you can see that the total amount of debt issued by the company has been far lower than the total debt it has repaid in the last few years. In 2023, the company issued ~$6.5 billion of debt but repaid a total of more than $8.8 billion, translating into net debt reduction of around $2.3 billion. The net reduction further expanded to about $3.2 billion in 2024 and almost $4 billion as of TTM 2025. With its above deleveraging goal for 2026, I expect a similar debt reduction in 2026. With its current market capitalization of around $117 billion, a $4 billion debt reduction would add a 3.41% effective yield towards the TSY and bring the total to be about 10.6%.

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of negatives, BTI (together with the rest of the tobacco sector) faces the secular decline of demand for its smokeable cigarettes. Also, judging by the FY2025 update, the contributions from its new products category remain limited (although the growth seems promising). In the meantime, the products in the new categories have been facing negative impacts from illicit products.

In terms of valuation, the picture is a bit mixed as you can see from Valuation Grade scorecard. BTI FWD P/E is around 11.6x, quite low in absolute terms or compared with the sector median of 15.28 and translating into a P/E discount of 23%. However, its current P/E is more than 35% above its own 5-year average. For me, I believe the current P/E is very attractive for at least two reasons. First, the headline P/E ignored the role of debt reduction as mentioned earlier. When valued in terms of EV, the multiples show far lower valuation premium compared to its historical norms as shown in the chart.

Second, I believe the current P/E ratio also underestimates its growth potential. As aforementioned, management guidance points to 5% to 8% growth rate on its adjusted EPS. As such, the stock’s PEG (P/E growth) ratio would be in a range of 1.4 to 2.3 only. When adjusted for the generous yield of 5.67%, the PEGY (P/E growth yield) ratio would be in a range of 0.85 to 1.09, quite close to the 1x ideal threshold.

To close, this BTI revisit focused on BTI’s FY25 trading update and its FY 2026 guidance. These updates have led me to see biased risk/reward ratio for several reasons. To start, I believe the current valuation understates its EPS growth potential. Several other specifics further skew the return/risk profile, such as the cash yield, buyback guidance, and deleverage targets. These factors point to a total shareholder yield of more than 10%, a downside cushion too wide to ignore.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lemonade's Cost Curve Experiment
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Lemonade Inc. (LMND) presents a compelling but fragile investment case. Its AI-driven platform is clearly reshaping the insurance cost structure, with tangible progress in lowering Loss Adjustment Expense (LAE), flattening OpEx, and compressing CAC as premiums scale, all pointing to meaningful operating leverage and future margin expansion.

Since my last coverage, LMND is up nearly 60%, and while I took profits in favor of better near-term opportunities, I still view LMND as an attractive long-term compounder. However, the stock’s premium valuation assumes near-flawless execution despite deeply negative margins and ongoing cash burn.

AI Platform Targets 50% LAE Reduction and Margin Expansion as Premiums Scale

When I analyze Lemonade's fundamentals, one most important element that stands out to me as bullish is the integration of its AI tech to create a cost structure different from traditional insurance carriers. This appears to be a systemic change to the business model, if scaled, may lead to high profitability. First, I examined the costs of handling claims or LAE. Lemonade’s LAE ratio is 7% relative to large carriers with ~9%. That 2%-point difference is meaningful on its own.

What really catches my attention is that Lemonade has already cut its LAE ratio in half over the past three years and may cut it in half again as the business doubles. This is linked to the AI OS blender, as it lets claim adjusters handle three times the claim volume they previously handled. This is about creating an operating model where the cost to service claims does not grow in direct proportion to the number of claims. Thus, as Lemonade grows, this expense line item should become a smaller and smaller percentage of premiums and expanding margins.

Second, I see this same element in Lemonade's general OpEx. Over the last two years, Lemonade’s gross profit has surged by over 260%, but its expenses jumped by single digits. Headcount is flat versus 24 months ago. To me, this is the clearest point of operating leverage. Lemonade is adding $439 million in in-force premium and more than doubling its gross profit without an increase in its employee base and other major operating costs.

This is a definition of turning variable and semi-variable costs into fixed costs. A traditional insurer expanding this quickly may almost certainly see a large increase in staffing for underwriting, support, and administrative, but Lemonade’s tech is absorbing that workload, meaning each incremental dollar of revenue is more profitable than the last.

Third, this tech approach extends to customer acquisition cost (CAC). For instance, in the car insurance segment, more than half of new customers are existing Lemonade customers from other products. This is a CAC-less acquisition, a negative CAC because these customers acquired for other profitable lines with better loss patterns and higher retention. This is important. Instead of heavy marketing to acquire every new car customer, Lemonade is leveraging its existing customer base and data to cross-sell to highly reduce the blended CAC. This may lead to scalable and highly profitable growth relative to topline growth.

Overall, in my view, these three areas (claims handling, general operations, and customer acquisition) have a constant pattern. Lemonade is using its tech for a better user interface and to fundamentally re-engineer the expense side. The model is built so that as Lemonade’s in-force premium grows from its current $1.16 billion toward a much larger number, the associated costs may grow at a far slower rate. This is a clear signal of accelerating profitability. With this built-in operating leverage, Lemonade may become positive at the bottomline sooner than Street analysts expect. As of now, PS ratio (TTM) is normalizing and contracting on a forward basis.

$5.6 Billion Valuation Prices Flawless Execution Despite –26% Net Margins

What concerns me is the severe disconnect between Lemonade’s stock valuation and its current negative profitability. This is not a minor overvaluation. It is a condition where LMND stock price seems to be discounted entirely on a story of its future success. This situation is very problematic as any execution misstep may shift market sentiment and lead to a sharp decline in the stock price. First, I look at the hard numbers on valuation. Lemonade has a forward Price/Book ratio of 11.7x.

This ratio has a premium of 800% over the sector median. Because Lemonade has no positive earnings, its P/E ratio is not meaningful as of now. It tells me that the LMND’s current stock price of $80 is supported by any traditional measure of value. It is purely a bet on future growth and a smooth transition to profitability. Lemonade’s market cap is $6.05 billion and yet it is not profitable. The stock's 12-month price performance has been 150%. It suggests that market expectations are already extremely high.

Second, this high valuation is set against a backdrop of very poor current profitability. I see an EBIT Margin of -24.91% and a Net Income Margin of -26.4%. This means for every dollar of topline Lemonade generates, it is losing ~$0.26 on the bottom line. Lemonade's operating cash over the last twelve months was negative $23.4 million. So, when management speaks confidently about a path to EBITDA profitability in Q4 2026, the present reality is that Lemonade’s business consumes cash and loses a great deal of money. Thus, the valuation is pricing in the destination, but Lemonade is still very much on the journey side, and as I see the journey is expensive.

Third, this is the most critical part for me, Lemonade's own operating philosophy introduces a source of volatility that may threaten the whole balance. The primary metric for Lemonade is gross profit dollars, instead of a low loss ratio. As per the CEO, "Given the choice, we will always privilege dollars over percentages," and that points that Lemonade may be profitable with a 90% loss ratio on certain CAC-less customers.

This approach of constantly optimizing price for demand elasticity means intentionally operating with higher loss ratios in some areas to maximize overall gross profit. This may be a sound long-term approach, to me it is also a high-wire act. Why? It increases the risk of a quarterly miss. If Lemonade's models miscalculate customer behavior and the loss ratio spikes more than expected without an offsetting revenue boost, the gross profit targets will be missed. And this is not good for the stock price.

For Lemonade, whose valuation is built on a perfect execution and a smooth path to profitability, it can be catastrophic. The high stock price has no value floor to hold in a fall. A disappointing quarter may cause a major reassessment of the entire bullish thesis that props up the valuation. Lemonade is also taking on more risk directly by reducing its quota share ceding rate. It is amplifying the impact of any underwriting errors.

Overall, the combination of an extremely high valuation, large current losses, and an operating model that willingly accepts higher loss ratios for growth creates a risky situation for LMND's stock price.

Takeaway

Lemonade’s AI platform is truly bending the cost curve for insurance, unlocking real operating leverage on the claim side, OpEx side, and acquisition side. If execution is right, margins can grow very quickly as the to-be-insured base increases. Currently, however, the market has already factored in that success.

data.title: Micron Technology: The AI Memory Chip Cash King To Buy
data.description:
Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) stock has surged 24% since my last report, outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), which remained more or less flat. In my previous report, I noted that memory price hikes and mix drive earnings upside, but the stock is expensive relative to its historic EV/EBITDA multiples. Micron reported fiscal Q1 2026 earnings in December, and in this report I discuss the earnings and update my price target, as I believe that the stock remains a Strong Buy.

Supply Chain Challenges And Mix Shift Drive Results

First-quarter revenues improved 20.6% sequentially and 56.7% year-on-year to $13.6 billion, beating analyst estimates by $760.7 million. Gross margins improved 110 basis points sequentially and 170 basis points year-on-year, while operating income grew 62.3% sequentially and 168.2% year-on-year. These are impressive growth numbers driven by higher shipments, a favorable mix, and supply chain challenges driving up prices. Average sales prices for DRAM grew 20% and accounted for 79% of the volume, whereas NAND represented 20% of sales and saw average sales price hikes in the mid-teens as solid-state storage demand is heating up.

In the Cloud Memory business, which closely aligns with the AI growth trends, sales doubled year-on-year and 16.3% sequentially, and, driven by higher prices and volumes, operating income grew 158.2% year-on-year and 30.1% sequentially, with operating income up 174.4% year-on-year and 33.3% sequentially.

The Core Data segment saw 3.8% year-on-year growth and 50.9% growth sequentially. Gross income increased 5.9% year-on-year and 87.7% sequentially, while operating income grew 1.1% year-on-year and 123.3% sequentially. Mobile and Client sales grew 63.2% year-on-year and 13.2% sequentially, with gross income increasing 226.3% year-on-year and 69.7% sequentially. Operating income grew 411.2% year-on-year and 83.4% sequentially. Auto & Embedded sales increased 48.5% year-on-year and 19.9% sequentially, while gross income increased 234.2% year-on-year and 74.1% sequentially. Operating income surged 663.9% year-on-year and 115.9% sequentially.

While the cloud memory growth is impressive, we note that across the board we are seeing significant revenue growth, and that is driven by supply chain constraints driving up memory prices, and we are also seeing that SSD demand is driving NAND pricing. So, a combination of supply chain constraints, a shift to premium high-bandwidth memory, and SSD demand is driving pricing strength across the business.

Micron Technology Provides Another Strong Guidance

For the fiscal second (current) quarter, Micron is guiding for $18.7 billion in sales plus or minus $400 million, and that points to 37% sequential growth, while growth margins are expected to be 67%-69%, providing another margin step up. EPS is expected to be $8.22-$8.62.

The growth is driven by strong average sales prices, and we note that HBM prices and volumes have been locked in for FY2026, meaning that there is no major pricing risk or opportunity for Micron. At the same time, node efficiencies are improving, which further increases the margins.

Micron Technology Stock Remains A Strong Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I previously had a base case target of $389.46, and I am now lowering that to $382.41, implying 13% upside with a bullish target of $420.34, implying 25% upside. While I am lowering the base case target, I believe the conviction on the Strong Buy rating is even higher, as the price target is no longer driven by expansion of the EV/EBITDA multiple but by a sharp increase in expected financial performance. EBITDA for this year has increased 42%, 53.4% for the year after, and 55.9% for 2028. The EBITDA increase in the years ahead is 51%, which is mind-blowing. The CAGR has improved from 29.9% to 51%. Free cash flow estimates rose 98.9%, with free cash flow expectations more than doubling for 2026 and 2027 and a 72.5% increase in 2028. The CAGR has improved from 104.8% to 145.7%.

EBITDA margin expectations previously were that EBITDA would peak at around 60% this year and then settle around 55% by 2028. However, the shortage of capacity to fulfill demand has caused a sharp increase for memory units across all classes, and as we are seeing that this does not lead to a reduction in demand and solutions to add capacity fast enough is a multi-year trajectory, the margins currently are expected to grow to 69% by 2028. It leads to a substantial improvement in the cash conversion rate to north of 40% and CapEx as a share of operating cash flow to decline from 90% to around 50%. I do believe that the surge in cash flow expectations also will lead to higher shareholder returns, and we note that even with the currently modelled $1.2 billion in annual share repurchases, the company will see its cash pile grow to $73.4 billion with a strong net cash position. We already saw that the company retired around $2.3 billion in debt early.

Conclusion: Micron Technology Outlook Is Simply Impressive

When I saw that Micron Technology stock advanced substantially compared to the price of publication of my prior report, I expected that there was a high probability that a rating downgrade needed to be applied. However, the supply chain bottleneck is driving up average sales prices to the point where EBITDA and free cash flow expectations have been increased by a percentage I have not seen before. It is reflective of the near- and mid-term reality that supply will remain constrained and that AI demand is here to stay for the time being. For that reason, I am maintaining my Strong Buy rating.

I believe that Micron Technology is executing extremely well, and while sequential growth will taper in Q1, the ramp-up of HBM4 should benefit the company in the second half of the year. Perhaps more important will be the demand and transition profile beyond 2026. That is where I currently see the first pressures, but it should also be noted that if data center spending continues to grow, EBITDA generation for 2027 could still be revised up.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks: Strong Execution, Stretched Valuation
data.description:
Palo Alto Networks, Inc.'s (PANW) business has been surprisingly resilient in 2025, supported by its XSIAM, SASE and software firewall products. This situation is likely to persist going forward due to AI demand, the nascent state of the SASE market and the emerging legacy SIEM replacement opportunity.

Palo Alto's planned CyberArk (CYBR) acquisition is also a potentially important development. I have written about CyberArk in the past, and believe it is the best positioned company in the identity security space. The importance of identity protection is likely to increase in coming years with the proliferation of agents, which is highlighted by CrowdStrike's (CRWD) recent decision to acquire SGNL.

I previously suggested that Palo Alto's stock was likely to offer limited upside in the near-term. The stock is down around 5% since then, with returns in 2025 particularly weak in comparison to the broader market. Palo Alto's distribution capabilities and comprehensive product portfolio should ensure it continues to have success. Valuation remains a concern, though, and likely means that returns will be closer to the market than the strong performance achieved between 2017 and 2024.

Market Conditions

AI is contributing to a resurgence of cloud transformation projects at the moment, supporting network security demand. Data security is also a hot area at the moment, due in large part to security concerns created by LLMs. While hardware is becoming a less important part of Palo Alto's business, appliance demand is also stable.

Palo Alto now estimates that it has a $200 billion TAM, which is expected to grow to $300 billion by 2028, supported by the additions of Chronosphere and CyberArk. This is largely made up of firewall, SASE, SOC and cloud security, which are all areas of strength for Palo Alto.

Palo Alto Business Updates

Like many companies in the cybersecurity space, much of Palo Alto's current focus is on automation, data and adapting to the changes brought by AI. This includes the recent acquisition of Chronosphere for approximately $3.35 billion. Chronosphere is an observability service provider with a scalable architecture that provides a cost advantage compared to other solutions on the market (~1/3 of the cost of other leading solutions). Chronosphere also recently acquired Calyptia, a data pipeline provider. This expands Palo Alto's business into areas like observability, security data pipelines and telemetry optimization. It also provides Palo Alto with broader observability capabilities than peers like CrowdStrike. While the price tag is steep, Chronosphere has around $160 million ARR and a triple-digit growth rate. Observability constitutes around 10-15% of infrastructure spend, and as a result, Palo Alto believes this could be an opportunity worth tens of billions of dollars. While current leaders include AppDynamics, Dynatrace and Datadog, Chronosphere is used by some of the large LLM providers.

The Chronosphere acquisition will also help Palo Alto to capitalize on the legacy SIEM replacement opportunity. Solutions from the likes of ArcSight, LogRhythm, QRadar and Splunk are outdated (lack of scalability, high costs, labor intensive) and are now in the process of being replaced. Palo Alto has roughly 470 XSIAM customers, and the average ARR per customer is over $1 million. This compares to 270 XSIAM customers in Q3 FY25 and 400 XSIAM customers in the fourth quarter. While this suggests a recent deceleration in the pace of customer acquisition, this is likely due to the easing of QRadar acquisition tailwinds.

Palo Alto has also acquired Protect AI, helping it to provide protection across the AI lifecycle. The acquisition forms the cornerstone of Prisma AIRS, Palo Alto's AI security platform.

In addition, Palo Alto recently launched Cortex AgentiX, which is the next generation of Cortex XSOAR. AgentiX provides a platform to build, deploy and govern agents for security and IT operations. Agents are an area where identity security is critical, and the adoption of GenAI and agents likely provided the impetus for Palo Alto's planned acquisition of CyberArk, a Privileged Access Management vendor, for approximately $25 billion (combination of cash and equity). While CyberArk had traded on rich multiples in recent years, it has a strong business and appears to be in the right place at the right time. AI is likely to increase the importance of identity, and Palo Alto has suggested that IAM doesn't provide the necessary security.

Palo Alto's SASE ARR increased 34% YoY in the first quarter, surpassing $1.3 billion. The company now has 6,800 SASE customers, including one third of the Fortune 500. While SASE strength appears broad-based, Palo Alto's secure browser is a growth driver. The company has around 1,200 secure browser customers and a cumulative total of more than 7.5 million browsers had been sold by the end of the first quarter. This is strategically important, as the majority of most white-collar employees work is performed in the browser.

While the businesses falling under Palo Alto's NGS banner are maturing, leading to more moderate growth, there is still a long growth runway. With the planned acquisitions, Palo Alto is now targeting $20 billion NGS ARR by FY2030. This is an optimistic target, though, which will require growth stabilizing in the mid 20% range. Growth continues to be driven by larger customers, with Palo Alto now having 169 customers with more than $5 million NGS ARR, up 54% YoY, and 55 customers with more than $10 million NGS ARR, up 49% YoY.

Quantum computing is also now being touted as a potential area of growth, with Palo Alto suggesting that its quantum security TAM is around $10 billion. This is based on a belief that quantum computing will be commercialized by 2029 and that companies need to start preparing now. In support of this, Palo Alto has introduced solutions to help companies take inventory of cryptographic risk and make legacy systems quantum safe. The company's Gen 5 firewalls are also optimized for quantum security. Palo Alto is also partnering with IBM to deliver solutions for post-quantum cryptography.

Financial Analysis

Palo Alto generated $2.47 billion of revenue in the first quarter, an increase of 16% YoY. RPO increased 24% YoY in the first quarter, although current RPO was only up 16%. NGS ARR totaled $5.85 billion, up 29% YoY. Product revenue grew 23% YoY, with around half of product revenue coming from software form factors. This part of the business is currently being supported by AI related demand. There remains a large opportunity here given that Palo Alto only has around 12,500 software firewall customers versus a total of 80,000 firewall customers.

NGS ARR growth in the second quarter is expected to be 28%, while full year NGS ARR growth is expected to be in the 26-27% range. Second quarter revenue is expected to be between $2.57 billion and $2.59 billion, an increase of 14% to 15%. For the full year, revenue is expected to be $10.50-$10.54 billion, up 14% YoY. While this suggests fairly stable growth through the year, the CyberArk acquisition is expected to close in fiscal Q3 and the Chronosphere acquisition is expected to close in the second half of the year.

Palo Alto's margins also continue to improve as its business matures. While the company's free cash flow margin is already high, this isn't really a good indicator of the underlying profitability of the business. Palo Alto has strong unit economics though, and its GAAP operating margin is likely to approach 30% at maturity. This is a positive, but it is already largely reflected in Palo Alto's revenue multiple, which is high for a company with a mid-teens growth rate.

Conclusion

Analysts expect Palo Alto to maintain a low double-digit revenue growth rate going forward. Identity, SIEM and observability are expected to be growth drivers going forward, and there remains a large SASE opportunity which Palo Alto is well positioned to capitalize on.

Including the CyberArk and Chronosphere acquisitions, Palo Alto is now targeting $20 billion NGS ARR by FY2030. This is a fairly aggressive target, though, which will require ARR growth to begin stabilizing in the mid 20% range. It would also probably only be sufficient for mid to low teen returns due to Palo Alto's relatively high revenue multiple.

While Palo Alto has a strong business that continues to perform well, there are reasons to be cautious beyond just valuation. XSIAM customer growth has stalled as Palo Alto has moved past the boost provided by QRadar. SASE customer growth is also moderating, which will contribute to an ongoing NGS growth slowdown, and platformizations decelerated sharply in the first quarter.

As a result, I believe that a number of cybersecurity companies are more attractive opportunities than Palo Alto at this point in time. This is supported by the fact that Palo Alto did not repurchase any shares in Q1, after stating its buyback strategy is opportunistic. Palo Alto's CTO has also sold a large number of shares in recent months, although this may not be that informative about the company's prospects.

data.title: Amazon: The Ultimate Nexus Of Growth Catalysts
data.description:
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) is significantly undervalued, as its positive earnings revisions significantly outpaced the modest 10% share price growth over the last twelve months. Despite underperformance compared to the S&P 500 Index (SP500) on a twelve-months horizon, the stock is gaining its momentum as it has significantly outperformed since October 26, when I shared my previous call. There were various positive recent developments around the company's most important businesses, the e-commerce and cloud infrastructure. Moreover, the forward P/E ratio setup is extremely bullish while Wall Street analysts believe AMZN is undervalued by 20%. AMZN remains a Strong Buy.

Fundamental analysis

Let's start with briefly summarizing Amazon's latest earnings release (Q3) since it was published after my previous call. Amazon's Q3 revenue grew by 13.4% on a year-over-year basis, which is impressive growth given that the last three years' revenue CAGR was 11.2%. From the EPS perspective standpoint, there was not only an impressive 36% year-over-year growth, but also a strong 25% positive surprise. Finally, the company demonstrated revenue strength across all businesses, as each segment delivered double-digit top line growth in Q3.

If we speak about the core business, e-commerce, Amazon remains the world's leader in this industry. It is a mature business, where future EPS growth mostly depends on the macro environment and cost-efficiency. From the macro environment perspective, the situation looks favorable for Amazon. The U.S. economy's growth in Q3, the latest available quarter, surprised economists with a 4.3% GDP growth compared to forecasted 3.2%. Furthermore, Fitch Ratings recently raised their GDP growth forecast for the U.S. for both 2025 and 2026.

There are other positive signs from the macro environment which are likely to be tailwinds for Amazon's e-commerce business. The country's labor market is still strong despite recent uptick in the unemployment rate, as it is still notably below long-term historical averages. Finally, despite all the fears of Spring 2025 regarding new tariffs' effect on inflation, the Fed does not plan to tighten monetary policy in 2026. With all that being said, the broader environment looks favorable for this business' revenue growth potential.

From the costs side perspective, key managers of this business are also working on streamlining operations. For example, the company strengthens partnerships with e-commerce logistics providers, like the one with Aeromexico Cargo. Besides partnerships, there is a crucial trend suggesting that despite aggressive growth of the company's e-commerce business after the pandemic, the headcount stayed approximately flat while the number of robots has almost doubled between 2022 and 2025. I see great potential to generate EPS gains from increasing the level of automatization, which will highly likely let the company to begin steadily cutting its 1.5 million headcount in the foreseeable future.

Amazon's second largest business, AWS, still leads in the global cloud infrastructure market with a 29% market share. Amazon's market share in the cloud market is larger than Microsoft's (MSFT) by nine percentage points, which is a solid gap. As the industry's undisputed leader, Amazon is extremely well positioned to capitalize on the projected 12.3% CAGR for the industry over the next decade. This industry is currently supercharged by AI tailwinds, where Amazon also succeeds.

Besides Amazon's leading position in cloud infrastructure industry, the company also boasts a solid network of partnerships to push AI innovation. Within just a few past days, Amazon inked a couple remarkable partnerships in the AI domain. The one with Infosys, aims to drive enterprise generative AI adoption via integration of Amazon's Q Developer with Infosys Topaz. It is extremely promising as the combined solution is designed to enhance processes spanning various use cases such as software development, HR, sales, automatic documentation, and vendor management.

Another interesting partnership inked by AWS recently is the one with Aumovio, a German technology and electronics company involved in development of autonomous vehicles. This partnership means that Amazon's cloud solutions are advanced and reliable enough to handle data processing for safety validation and real-world AI. The intersection of AI and automotive industries is one of the most promising mega-trends of the modern era as the autonomous vehicle market is expected to deliver a 35% CAGR over the next decade.

Besides direct involvement of AWS in AI-related projects, let's not forget that Amazon is one of the major investors in Anthropic, one of the hottest private generative AI companies in the world. This generative AI startup reportedly plans to raise $10 billion at $350 valuation. The previous round took place in early September when Anthropic was valued at $183 billion. Almost a 2x increase in Anthropic's (ANTHRO) valuation means Amazon will record hefty capital gains from this investment in 2026.

To summarize, with so many positive developments around the company's two major businesses, I think that maintaining bullish outlook is reasonable. Moreover, Amazon appears substantially undervalued at the moment.

Valuation analysis

Amazon's forward P/E setup is extremely bullish as the multiple is expected to almost half by FY2028. I believe that this forecast is reasonable because developments around Amazon's fundamentals demonstrate that future EPS growth will be supported by both revenue growth and keeping costs under control. Moreover, Amazon has stellar EPS growth record with a staggering 70% CAGR over the last decade. Amazon's estimated FY2028 forward P/E ratio of 19 looks extremely attractive considering the company's leadership in both cloud infrastructure and e-commerce, both industries expected to demonstrate double-digit CAGR over the next several years.

My assertion that AMZN is very attractively valued is also confirmed by Wall Street analysts. The average target price for AMZN is $296, which means a 20% upside from AMZN's current share price. This upside alone is already compelling for a stock like AMZN. However, I think there are great chances that this target price might see upward revisions. Wall Street's consensus price directly depends on EPS revisions, and Amazon's stellar fundamentals increase chances for new EPS forecast upgrades.

Moreover, there is strong momentum in EPS revisions. This can be seen from the above schedule with Amazon's all positive EPS revisions for several years ahead over the last six months. With rock-solid fundamentals and robust earnings revisions momentum, I think that we are likely to see even more aggressive P/E ratio compression trajectory.

Mitigating factors

While share prices tend to follow fundamentals over the long-term, there might be sentiment-driven short-term share price declines. AMZN gained approximately 11% over the last twelve months, significantly underperforming compared to the S&P 500 Index (SP500) and many other tech stocks with AI exposure. AMZN's relatively weak share price momentum might be the reason why investors would prefer to ignore this stock for some time. It might take a few quarters with massive earnings beats to regain investors' confidence. Therefore, my bullish thesis is for long-term investors. People who want to try to speculate or time the market might get disappointed at the end of the day.

Despite all apparent fundamental positives and attractive valuation, insiders are not rushing to buy the stock. The fact that insiders have been only selling AMZN over the last several months might become another notable share price headwind if they keep selling aggressively. For example, the public might consider continued insider selling as indication that there is limited upside.

Conclusion

AMZN looks extremely attractively valued at the moment, and I tend to agree with the double-digit upside projected by Wall Street analysts. I am highly confident because the management is driving EPS growth not only by pushing revenue expansion but by keeping costs under strict control. With such a comprehensive approach, I believe AMZN will demonstrate solid EPS growth and revisions over the next several quarters.
